findings.description_verbatim: Remote verbal assessments are happening over the phone for patients that regularly visit your pharmacy for things like clinical follow ups and prescription extensions. We discussed some of the basic requirements to consider for this type of virtual care, including: 1. Identifying yourself and your role 2. Confirming the identity of the patient 3. Ensuring the patient is in a private setting 4. Obtaining informed consent from the patient to proceed with sharing patient information over the phone (this should be documented in the patient record) 5. Documenting the consent in the patient record 6. Provide the patient with contact information for follow up In some of the examples we reviewed, a few of the elements outlined above - such as documenting patient consent for virtual care - were inconsistently documented on the patient's record. Please review these requirements with all team members and monitor these activities for compliance moving forward. We will review a few more virtual care examples at the next assessment.

category.raw: Operations : Integrity and Safety of Drugs

findings.description_verbatim: When documenting the outcomes of your investigation into narcotic discrepancies, ensure sufficient detail is recorded for future reference. For instance, record the prescription number from the transaction that was determined to have contributed to a discrepancy. Also as discussed, please ensure the method of denaturation is consistently documented on the record for controlled drug destruction activities moving forward.

1. Review the following reference regarding emergency anaphylaxis kits and written protocols for managing these situations. Note that the recommended epinephrine dose for individuals weighing 45kg or more is 0.5mg. Ensure you obtain and stock sufficient products to manage all dosing requirements of your patient (and your updated your policies to reflect this recommendation). Post the dose administration guide in your counseling room for easy reference. https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/publications/healthy-living/canadian- immunization-guide-part-2-vaccine-safety/page-4-early-vaccine-reactions-including- anaphylaxis.html 2. Ensure your needlestick injury protocol is readily available in your private counselling rooms. See here for a printable algorithm and other materials: https://scp.in1touch.org/document/3633/ REF_Needlestick_Injury.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

At the time of this assessment, you did not have any active patients receiving opioid agonist therapy at your pharmacy. We will continue to review the status of such services at upcoming assessments to ensure compliance with relevant standards and guidelines. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed Standards of Practice related to informed consent for physical contact with patients when administering certain injections (e.g. ventrogluteal, abdominal, etc). You indicated that pharmacists are occasionally administering abdominal injections at your pharmacy, so the following consent principles are important to review and discuss with your patients: - evidence that the patient has given informed consent about the nature, purpose, and duration of the contact required for this injection; - evidence that the patient is offered appropriate privacy for the physical contact that occurs; - evidence that the patient has given informed consent as to the room within which the regulated member will be providing the professional service that involves physical contact; - evidence that the patient is offered a to have a chaperon present during the contact. Share these requirements with your team and ensure they are being utilized in such situations moving forward.

In reviewing some of the prescription adaptations completed by pharmacists today, it was noted that aspects of the patient's chief complaint (or history of present illness) was not always documented on the record. For instance, one example involved the dose adaptation of a patient's azithromycin for community acquired pneumonia. However, the pharmacist's assessment of this indication, inclusive of the patient's symptoms and treatment history, was not recorded in the documentation note. We discussed the expectation that pharmacists document their own thorough assessment with each prescription adaptation or extension. Use the Chat Check Chart adaptation tool to ensure your documentation includes at a minimum: - history of present illness including timelines of symptoms and locations of symptoms - previous use of medications including dates, timelines, and outcomes - relevant Netcare data, including labwork (indicate what information on Netcare was consulted) - differential diagnosis considerations - alternatives considered by the pharmacist Have a few examples from each pharmacist to discuss at the next visit. https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/PrescriptionAdaptationGuide.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed new standards pertaining to equity, diversity, inclusion (EDI); accessibility; cultural sensitivity, and Indigenous cultural safety (SPPPT domain 1). Each pharmacy needs to have standard operating procedures (SOPs) to support the pharmacy team in incorporating these concepts into practice. We discussed some patient groups who may be subject to barriers to care due to prejudice, physical or cognitive barriers, etc. Some key considerations for you to consider include: -Changes that can be made in the pharmacy to be more inclusive for these groups -Changes to make the space more welcoming (e.g., language, pharmacy appearance, physical environment) -Staff training and ongoing monitoring to ensure implementation of these changes. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/treating-people-with-respect-and-dignity/ Encourage team members to review the article in the Link above and begin to reflect on your current approaches for meeting EDI, accessibility, cultural sensitivity and Indigenous cultural safety expectations in your pharmacy.

We reviewed several care plans where general goals of therapy were documented on the record. For instance, a common goal of therapy documented for patients taking anti-hypertensives was "BP < 140/90 or BP <130/80 for Diabetes." We reviewed the importance of clarifying and documenting mutual goals of therapy with patients to ensure therapeutic assessment and monitoring activities are patient-specific. Considering this, ensure to collect and document the patient's specific goal(s) of therapy on the record beginning with your next care plan (or other care intervention such as initial access prescription, adaptation, etc.). Use these goals to guide your assessment, care planning, and follow up process and set aside a few examples demonstrating this activity for us to review together at the next visit. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

category.raw: Pharmacy Practice : Monitoring and Follow- up

findings.description_verbatim: We reviewed several prescribing examples (initial access, adaptation, extension) where follow up planning was somewhat general and unclear. For instance, one example involved a pharmacist prescribing valacyclovir for a patient where it was indicated that the patient "is aware to follow-up with the pharmacy with questions." However, we discussed the expectation that pharmacists develop and implement monitoring and follow up plans with patients for whom they've prescribed, and that delegating this activity to the patient is not always appropriate. With this in mind, discuss with your team the requirement that a documented and patient-specific monitoring plan is included within the summary note for prescribing interventions. The monitoring plan should identify who is following up, when this is expected to occur, and what parameters are being assessed at that time. Further, aim to use the Kroll follow up system more consistently to keep track of follow up activities across all pharmacists. Set aside some examples of follow up care completed by your pharmacists using this system for us to review at the next assessment. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

As per Standard 5.5.1 (https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ACP_SOLP.pdf) A licensee must ensure the following with respect to digital records: a) ensure all data required under the legislative framework is backed up at least once daily; b) store a copy of the backup data a. at an approved off-site location, b. using a secure cloud-based service provider that stores data within Canada, c. in a fire- and theft-resistant safe; c) ensure the backup is stored so that it is retrievable in the event the system malfunctions or is destroyed; d) ensure the backup is kept securely to avoid theft or unauthorized access, use, or disclosure; and e) ensure there is a disaster recovery plan in place as part of the policies and procedures of the pharmacy to restore patient data and systems as quickly as possible in the event of a data loss or system failure At the time of the assessment, it was not clear how your pharmacy complied with these requirements. As such, within the next 30 days, take steps to confirm the processes in place for backing up required information in your pharmacy and document these policies for the awareness of all staff.

For care plans, ensure your documentation includes evidence that a CHECK (Using the Chat, Check, Chart model) assessment has been performed for all medications on the best possible medication history list. Ensure that you establish a follow-up plan with patient specific monitoring parameters for each identified drug therapy problem and medical condition (even if the full work up will occur at a subsequent visit). Ensure you document when follow-up will occur and who will complete it. Aim to have one new care plan or care plan follow-up to discuss at the next visit. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed that the standards of practice and legislative framework do not permit pharmacies to act as a "depot" for prescriptions to be delivered to. We discussed that mail order pharmacies must deliver prescriptions to patients directly, and not through other pharmacies that are located closer to your patients. You indicated that your site has been asked by certain mail order pharmacies to receive and store refrigerated medications in cases where they cannot deliver directly to the patient. Prior to my next assessment, make contact with these pharmacies to advise them that this practice must cease, and they must either deliver drugs directly to the patient, or transfer the prescription to the patient's nearest pharmacy.

category.raw: Laboratory and Point- of-Care Testing : Point- of-Care Testing Program

findings.description_verbatim: Within the next 30 days, please update your policies to better define your strep testing procedures. Specifically, outline a step-by-step guide that addresses the following: • Screening and assessment activities • Calibration of equipment (instrument/reagent) as required by the manufacturer. • Training requirements and procedures. I would suggest that training should also include a review of the device user manual(s). • Personal protective equipment (PPE). Your SOP should include information regarding mask usage. • Establishment of acceptable target or reference ranges for QC materials. For example, control testing instructions. We will review this again together at the next assessment.
Beginning with the next narcotic count, ensure an audit trail is recorded indicating any personnel involved in the count, as well as the pharmacist who oversaw the activity.

Review the following resources regarding the handling and administration recommendations for hazardous drug injections in the pharmacy. - NIOSH Reference: https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/docs/2016-161/pdfs/2016-161.pdf?id=10.26616/ NIOSHPUB2016161 (see Table 5 on Page 32 for specific recommendations) - ACP Guidelines on Injection Safety: https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ Guidelines_Injection_Safety.pdf (see Section 6 for specific guidance) Review your own procedures for hazardous injectables to ensure the following considerations are in place: - Hazardous drugs are stored separately from other medications if possible. - Employees and others at risk from handling hazardous drugs are identified and provided with adequate training and equipment - Cytotoxic spill kits are available and staff are trained in spill management - Adequate personal protective equipment including but not limited to gloves and facial protection is worn for administration of hazardous drugs. Examples of gloves are here: https:// www.medisca.ca/products/nitrile-gloves-9-4mil - Additional personal protective equipment is used where there is potential for splash, spill or aerosolization. Personal protective equipment is disposable where possible. - Hazardous drug waste and equipment is disposed at point‐of‐use into a cytotoxic waste container with minimal manipulation (e.g., needles and syringes are left intact). Document and implement your own procedures for managing and administering these products by injection in your pharmacy. Aim to have this completed by the next assessment.

With respect to homeopathic products, please implement a plan regarding the standard that requires drugs to be displayed and advertised independently of such products. This can be accomplished by means of physical separation or signage such that a member of the public can easily distinguish drugs in the pharmacy from any homeopathic products.

We also discussed the use of your "paperless" Kroll System, whereby the processing of a prescription is broken down into several steps and several people are involved in the filling of the prescription. Components of the audit trail for dispensed prescriptions are recorded electronically in Kroll with no accompanying written record or written/electronic signature of the individual involved in filling or packaging tasks. This system relies on adherence to policies and procedures to maintain the accuracy of the audit trail including the following features: - ensuring there is an “auto lock” or "auto log out” feature for workstations that have been inactive for a period of time - ensuring your policies indicate that staff log out of their workstations when moving to perform other tasks - the policy that staff attempt to use the same workstations and avoid sharing terminals as much as possible. Continue to analyze and reassess your system and procedures to ensure an accurate and complete audit trail is being maintained for all prescriptions dispensed by your pharmacy.

We discussed the requirement to develop and document a plan to ensure continuity of patient care in the event planned or unplanned pharmacy closures and the loss or loss of access to pharmacy records. At the time of this assessment, you weren't sure if corporate policies were available that would provide guidance to your pharmacy team during a disaster. Prior to my next assessment, please contact the appropriate party to determine if such policies are in place. We will review progress on this item at the next assessment. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

I have included relevant links below to support our discussion at the next visit. Please ensure to review relevant links to support your implementation of the program. Please note that policies and procedures should be updated to support continuous quality improvement for the licensed pharmacy. Link Article: https://abpharmacy.ca/news/acp-launches-cqi/ Q&A: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CQI_QA.pdf Guide to CQI+: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CQI_Guide.pdf CQI+ Quick-start Checklist: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CQI_Checklist.pdf CQI+ Program Requirement: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/overview/program- requirements/ CQI+ Platform Requirement: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/overview/platform- requirements/ Mandatory Reporting to the NIDR: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/overview/ mandatory-reporting-to-the-nidr/ Relevant Resources: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/resources/

You've recently installed a new Kroll operating system, which allows staff to record lot numbers and expiry dates within the transaction. category.raw: Non-sterile Compounding : Training and Skills Assessment

findings.description_verbatim: In order to maintain requirements to perform Level A compounding, you must administer and maintain a training and skills assessment program. There are two distinct components of training requirements for non-sterile compounding: theoretical training and skills assessments. The training component of the program covers knowledge-related aspects of non-sterile compounding as well as the policies and procedures in place at the pharmacy. The skills assessment evaluates whether compounding personnel apply their knowledge correctly and prepare products in compliance with the Standards. Prior to my next assessment, please review Table 1 in Section 5.2 of the nonsterile compounding standards at the following link to compare your existing training and skills assessment program with the all required items. This procedure must include a tracking sheet or record for all personnel that have completed the assessment and must also be repeated annually. We will review progress on both items at the next assessment. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidance_Non-sterile_Compounding.pdf

In order to maintain foundational expectations for Level A nonsterile compounding, you must have a process for conducting and documenting master formulation records. A master formulation record must be completed for each compounded product to ensure an appropriate process and equipment is followed to prepare the medication. Master Formulation Records for compounded preparations must be reviewed and updated at least annually as well. With this in mind, please develop and document your procedure for updating your master formulation records moving forward. Aim to have this completed and implemented for two of your more common compounded preparations within the next 30 days. As discussed, the key considerations to include in the updated formulation record are: - a reputable formula to base your process on - the required equipment for the preparation - the required active ingredients for the preparation - the process staff are to follow for preparing the compound An example formulation record is available here: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ Compounding_TemplateMasterFormulationRecord.pdf

In order to maintain requirements to perform Level A compounding, you must maintain policies and procedures describing your site-specific preparation processes. The policies should include: - the types/level of compounding that will occur in your pharmacy - the names and contact information of your contracted compounding and repackaging pharmacies (including ordering procedures) - the process for compound entry in the patient file (including audit trail tracking details using the compound lot or prescription number) - the process for accessing SDS information for APIs - the process for completing and documenting risk assessments - the process for completing and documenting master formulation records Aim to begin documenting your procedures prior to my next assessment in 6 weeks.

In order to maintain foundational expectations for Level A nonsterile compounding, you must have a process for conducting and documenting risk assessments. A risk assessment must be completed for each compounded product to ensure appropriate equipment, clothing, and processes are followed for preparing the medication. Risk assessments for compounded preparations must be reviewed and updated at least annually as well. With this in mind, please develop and document your procedure for updating your risk assessments moving forward. Aim to have this completed and implemented for two of your more common compounded preparations within the next 30 days. As discussed, the key considerations to include in the updated risk assessment are: - assessment of the frequency of preparing the compound - assessment of the quantities prepared for each compound - assessment of the concentrations of ingredients used in the compound - assessment of the cumulative risk of exposure for the ingredients in the compound These factors can be considered in comparison to the previous risk assessment documented for each compound to determine if changes to your practice or infrastructure are needed. Ensure the risk assessment is documented to include a revision date and audit trail of the compounding personnel who completed the review. The following link includes an example of a risk assessment document: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp- content/uploads/Compounding_Risk_assessment_2.pdf

As discussed, the licensee must ensure drug error documentation is reviewed at least quarterly to evaluate whether practice changes or preventive measures are required to prevent future drug errors, and to assess whether any changes implemented as a result of a drug error were successful in advancing patient safety. In the absence of medication errors, begin documenting close calls and incorporating this data into your quarterly review. When assessing close calls or near misses, aim to identify key behaviors that prevented the event from becoming an error. For instance, if showing a patient their medication at pick up resulted in the identification of a near miss, note this key activity in your quarterly review summary The quarterly review must be documented and reviewed with all staff. Use the following resources to complete your quarterly review of drug errors for the first quarter of 2025. We will review this together at the next assessment. 1. Quarterly review report template: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ DrugIncidentQuarterlyReview_Fillable.pdf 2. Sample of completed quarterly review report: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ DrugIncidentQuarterlyReview_Sample.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date
Please complete and document the training and skills assessments for relevant nonsterile compounding personnel (likely yourself and Sean) prior to my next assessment.

You and your team have made great progress at implementing the practice goals we set at the previous assessment. Continue to focus on the following areas to maintain momentum and to ensure practices are applied consistently: - indications are consistently being confirmed at intake and documented on the record - ensuring a 'check' assessment is performed for all medications on the patient's BPMH - patient-specific details (such as onset of symptoms, aggravating/remitting factors, etc) are documented on the record for prescribing decisions - Netcare information is consulted and documented on the record for relevant clinical decisions - collaborative notes regarding your interventions for patients get consistently shared with relevant health care providers - a reference or guidelines is used to support initial prescribing decisions - a clear and patient-specific monitoring and follow up plan is documented with each assessment summary - assessment information related to ongoing injections (B12) is routinely documented on the record of care - patient consent is clearly documented on the record for virtual care activities Please set aside a few will review a few more patient care activities (including APA cases) at the next assessment.

Schedule 2 products must be stored in the dispensary and be dispensed by a pharmacist. Immediately ensure all scheduled products are in the appropriate locations. I observed the beyond use dates of some compounds, PPIs for example, being extended. Unless you can verify that your equipment, ingredients, and procedures precisely match those that are indicated within the supplied Master Formulation Record, you must defer to the ACP Standard BUDs referenced here: https://abpharmacy.ca/articles/does-your-pharmacy-compound-non-sterile- topical-preparations-such-diclofenac-gel Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

A delivery log must be in place for medications that are not picked up by the patient or their agent. We discussed that the requirements for this log is updated with the new standards in effect in February 2025. Pasted below for your convenience, ensure that you have a delivery log in place: "ensure there is documentation for each delivered drug that includes i. the shipping date, date received, shipping address, name of recipient, name of carrier, and environmental controls undertaken; and ii. an audit trail that identifies A. the prescription number of each drug delivered, B. all individuals who were involved in the delivery of the drug, C. the role of each individual involved in the delivery of the drug, and D. confirmation of receipt by the patient or authorized receiver" New SOLP 4.51 https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_SOLP_New.pdf

I observed unlabeled syringes containing methadone on the counter and a bottle of methadone in which the larger stock bottle had been repackaged into without complete labelling. All medications must be labelled in the pharmacy, including products that have been repackaged. In addition, when not being used at the time for dispensing, these products must be stored in the time delayed safe. Standard 21 outlines the requirements, pasted below: A pharmacist or a pharmacy technician who repackages a drug or blood product must ensure that in respect of that drug or blood product there is sufficient documentation to provide a clear audit trail of the repackaging process. 21.2 The documentation required under Standard 21.1 must identify: a) drug information from the original container including: i. DIN, NPN or HN; ii. lot number; iii. expiry date; and b) all individuals involved in the repackaging and verification process and the role of each individual The pharmacy is not maintaining a methadone spill log. Review the "methadone preparations" section of this Health Canada document: https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/health-concerns/controlled-substances- precursor-chemicals/controlled-substances/compliance-monitoring/compliance-monitoring- controlled-substances/guidance-community-pharmicists-security-inventory-reconciliation-record- keeping.html Take steps to ensure that any potential methadone loss is documented and retained. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

findings.description_verbatim: We discussed cleaning activities within the compounding space. 1. The pharmacy is performing hazardous compounding, but does not have agents required to decontaminate and deactivate the surfaces; currently there is only cleaning and disinfection occuring. Review section 9.3 of the Non-Sterile Compounding Guidance Document to ensure familiarity with the expectations for the cleaning activities in the compounding room and C-PEC. Ensure that you have proper agents to clean this area properly and at the proper intervals. Ensure that this is incorporated into your cleaning log. 2. There are hard to clean areas in the compounding room that require attention as there is visible dirt and contamination, such as the seam on the floor and the drying rack. Take time to ensure all hard to clean areas are cleaned appropriately and incorporate cleaning of these hard to clean areas into the policies and procedures as well as the cleaning log. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidance_Non-sterile_Compounding.pdf

Temperature and humidity should be monitored in the compounding room. Create a log to track these daily. The temperature of the room should be less than or equal to 20°C, taking into account employees' comfort once all PPE has been donned. 9.1.2 & 5.4.1.3 https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ Guidance_Pharmacy_Non_sterile_Compounding.pdf

findings.description_verbatim: Specimen collection is occurring for the practice of identifying patients with Hepatitis C, but the policies and procedures were not available for this activity. Ensure that test specific SOPs are available an that staff performing this test are trained and this training is retained. Review the standards specific to specimen collection (28-32) and we will discuss this activity further at the next visit. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_Lab_POCT.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

The pharmacy is conducting hazardous compounding but practices for personnel safety are not occurring consistently Section 9 of the Guidance Document (https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/ files/Guidance_Pharmacy_Non-sterile_Compounding.pdf) should be reviewed to ensure that PPE, cleaning, handling, and disposal of hazardous products is met. 1. Ensure your pharmacy has appropriate PPE (gloves, gowns, etc) based on the risk assessment of your hazardous compounds. I observed that the pharmacy had booties and hazardous gowns, but these were not initially donned for the preparation of a progesterone compound. In addition, the pharmacy does not have chemo-protective gloves available. 2. Ensure hazardous waste is disposed of properly 3. Ensure your cleaning policies and procedures have safety protocols in place for personnel, including proper PPE and a spill kit on hand. At the time of the visit, the spill kit was not available as it had been used recently 4. When using reusable equipment, make effort to designate equipment for hazardous use only 5. Although we did not discuss wipe sampling during the visit, this should be completed at least twice annually to assess for hazardous product contamination in the compounding area and dispensary (9.6.3)

Pharmacists should review and sign a two-way agreement with the patient receiving OAT. Currently the pharmacy has a referral form for patients at the drop in center, but we discussed reviewing the ACP template to determine if items could be incorporated into this existing document. This agreement should be kept on the patient's record, and revisited/updated regularly as treatment goals evolve. 5.5 https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidelines_OAT.pdf

category.raw: Operations : Integrity and Safety of Drugs

findings.description_verbatim: We discussed inventory management of narcotics/controlled/targeted medications. During our discussion, you mentioned that you try to complete this full count on a quarterly basis, but this is not always done consistently. I also observed the last count document, and not all narcotics, controlled and targeted medications were included. There were multiple discrepancies (overages and shortages) without a documented investigation. The latest destruction log was not retrievable. Over the next month: 1. Ensure all narcotics, controlled and targeted medications have been counted within the last 3 months 2. Investigate any and all discrepancies and document your findings 3. Report any unexplained loss to Health Canada within 10 days of discovery 4. Retrieve the destruction report and ensure it is kept for 10 years We will review your inventory count and associated documentation at the next visit.

There are pharmacies that receive compounds from your pharmacy through the Compounding and Repackaging (C&R) licence, but you are in the process of updating the agreements. Ensure that compounds are not dispensed to a pharmacy unless the C&R agreement is current and retrievable. In addition, we discussed that your pharmacy does not have a Health Canada Dealer's Licence, and therefore dispensation of controlled substances to other pharmacies is not permitted unless there is an emergency. "Emergency means a circumstance where a patient urgently requires a professional service that includes a restricted activity for the purposes of preventing imminent mortality or morbidity." and we discussed that it would not apply to compounds such as tramadol. Ensure that dispensation of controlled substances to other pharmacies ceases unless the pharmacy has a Dealer's Licence. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/controlled-drugs-and-substances-dealers-licence-requirement/ Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

There are some companion animal patients that receive medications from the pharmacy. Ensure that the label of these medications that are dispensed to animal patients indicate the species and that it is for "veterinary use". The animal profile must also have the name of the patient agent.

Since the last visit, the pharmacy has rearranged the pharmacy workflow and barriers within the pharmacy. There is now a stanchion between the front entrance and the consultation room meant to prevent patient access to the consultation room. Once past this stanchion, there is not a barrier preventing access into the dispensary. Also, the pick up and drop off are now at the same counter instead of the separate areas depicted on the last submitted floorplan. The consultation room is a patient area and must be accessible to the patients. The dispensary must have an effective physical barrier preventing access to this area. Within 30 days, submit a renovation application with a layout that allows the private consultation room to be accessible from the patient services area as well as a dispensary with an effective physical barrier in place allowing for separation from the dispensary and patient services area. Note, a stanchion is not an effective physical barrier for this purpose. In addition, the floorplan must match the layout of the pharmacy (eg. drop off/pick up counters). The renovation assessment will also have to detail the plans to convert the level B compounding room to a level C compounding room, however this will be detailed in a different deficiency.

The pharmacy does not have all required references. Ensure that you have the following references from the approved list: - Medical Dictionary - Dispensatory/Foreign Drug Reference -Natural Health Products and Alternative Therapies Reference https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/RequiredReferences.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

The compounding supervisor must have training and skills assessment done, along with any staff involved in compounding. Ensure that training/skills assessment is updated for all compounding staff and is completed for the compounding supervisor at a minimum. This link may be helpful https://www.napra.ca/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/NAPRA-Mdl-Stnds- Pharmacy-Compounding-Nonsterile-Preparations-Guidance-Fillable-Printable-Forms-EN-Final.pdf but training should be site specific

I observed the pharmacy compounding numerous compounds (approximately 10-12 compounds) a day. Included in this quantity, there are numerous compounds with ingredients on the NIOSH list and with WHIMIS health hazards made in batches and multiple varieties. Not only is the pharmacy compounding for patients of the pharmacy, they are increasing their volume of compounds by compounding for pharmacies within their network through a compounding and repackaging licence. There has also been an application submitted for a Health Canada Dealer's Licence which may further increase volume at this site. I am concerned about the safety of compounding staff at this location with the current infrastructure and environmental controls. The compounding room is currently designed for the practice of compounding at a Level B capacity, however, the pharmacy's compounding activities with respect to volume and types of compounds exceeds that and a renovation to a level C compounding room must occur for the current practice of compounding to proceed. The compounding room must be renovated to meet Level C requirements, as described in the Guidance Document for Pharmacy Compounding of Non-Sterile Preparations, • Sink must be at least 1 meter away from C-PEC, • The compounding room must be well-ventilated with external venting through high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filtration, maintain negative pressure (-2.5 Pa relative to surrounding areas) and at least 12 air changes per hour • The room must include heating, ventilation and air conditioning systems that are controlled to avoid decomposition and contamination of chemicals, to maintain the quality of products, and to ensure the safety and comfort of compounding personnel • Air vents cannot be directly over work areas • The C-PEC is installed in the compounding room should either be externally vented (the preferred option) or have redundant HEPA filters in a series Within 30 days, submit a renovation application with your plans to modify the compounding room as well as the pharmacy space (outlined in another deficiency).

Methadone is measured with oral syringes. The OAT guidelines, section 8.4, (https:// abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/OAT_Guidelines.pdf) indicates that the measurement of methadone must be done with calibrated equipment with an error rate no greater than +/- 0.1mL. At the time of the assessment, I observed staff utilizing a 20 mL syringe to measure 15.5 mL of methadone for which there was not graduation appropriate for this measurement. In addition, we reviewed the accuracy data for the syringes that are being used and syringes greater than 5 mL of this brand to not fall in the required accuracy window. Immediately, ensure syringes without a proper error rate are not used. A new method must be considered when measuring these volumes. Note that syringes may not be reused.

Aim to have this completed by the next assessment, and ensure the following items are addressed in the procedure: - Hazardous drugs are stored separately from other medications if possible. - Employees and others at risk from handling hazardous drugs are identified and provided with adequate training and equipment - Cytotoxic spill kits are available and staff are trained in spill management - Adequate personal protective equipment including but not limited to gloves, disposable gowns and facial protection is worn for administration of hazardous drugs. - Additional personal protective equipment is used where there is potential for splash, spill or aerosolization. Personal protective equipment is disposable where possible. - Hazardous drug waste and equipment is disposed at point‐of‐use into a cytotoxic waste container with minimal manipulation (e.g., needles and syringes are left intact).

In addition to the labelling requirements outlined in the Standards of Practice for Pharmacists and Pharmacy Technicians, further labelling requirements for take-home methadone doses include: • The total milligrams of methadone to be ingested in a single dose. • Instructions to consume the entire contents of the bottle. • The date on which the contents of each individual bottle should be ingested as a single dose. This ingestion date should be clearly distinct from the date of dispensing or other dates on the bottle (e.g., prescription expiry date, BUD of the methadone, etc.). Example: Drug is already diluted. Drink the entire contents of this bottle (X mg) on [Insert Date] Please also ensure that all of the following auxiliary labels are used for take-home methadone doses: • “Methadone may cause serious harm to someone other than the intended patient. Not to be used by anyone other than the patient for whom it was intended. May be fatal to a child or adult.” OR “May be toxic or lethal if ingested by a child or adult other than the intended patient. Accidental ingestion is considered a medical emergency and requires immediate medical attention.” • A “Keep refrigerated” label (if diluted). • A beyond use date of the prepared bottle (if diluted). Please review the above labelling requirements

Sections 8.9 and 8.9.2 of the Opioid Agonist Therapy (OAT) Guidelines outline the labelling requirements for take-home methadone doses. As discussed, your 'take-home dose’ labels are not compliant to this guideline. The following should be included: • The total milligrams of methadone to be ingested in a single dose. • Instructions to consume the entire contents of the bottle. • The date on which the contents of each individual bottle should be ingested as a single dose. This ingestion date should be clearly distinct from the date of dispensing or other dates on the bottle (e.g., prescription expiry date, BUD of the methadone, etc.). There is an example of a label in this OAT section. I have also included another example below. "Drug is diluted with Tang. Consume the ENTIRE contents of bottle (XXXmg) once daily. Date: ________ (Prescription active from July 12 to August 6).” At my next visit, we will review your updates the Methadone label. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidelines_OAT.pdf

findings.description_verbatim: We discussed the importance of a comprehensive follow-up/monitoring plan when providing “patient/person-center care”. Standard 7.9.1 of the SPPPT outlines the requirements of regulated members when developing a monitoring and follow-up plan. When developing a monitoring and follow-up plan, consider the following: WHO – is responsible for the follow-up? (i.e., which pharmacist will follow-up) WHAT – specific parameters will be discussed (i.e., efficacy, safety, adherence, rationale, etc.) WHEN – will the follow-up take place? (i.e., within a few days, next month, next refill, etc.) For example: RPh to call patient in 7 days to confirm if dizziness has reduced with the dose reduction of ramipril. Your documentation for the monitoring and follow-up plan should include the WHO, WHAT, and WHEN as we discussed. Starting with your next care activity, ensure you have reviewed Standard 7.9 and are developing a monitoring and follow-up plan to support your processes in providing patient care.

As discussed, pharmacists must collect information and identify treatment options that are indicated, likely to be effective, safe, and something the patient is willing and able to adhere to (aka IESA). Upon review of the cases presented today, there was inconsistency with the confirmation of IESA prior to preforming a restricted activity. It is required that sufficient information is collected from a patient to support patient care and that the information is documented in the patient record. As per Standard 7.4 of the SPPPT, a pharmacist meets patients' health needs and goals by developing plans for patient care, which includes collecting information and identifying treatment options by applying IESA, as reflected within Standards 7.4.1(a) and 7.4.1(b). Below is an example documentation note that demonstrates the appropriate collection of information: D: Pt presents to the pharmacy requesting a refill of Synthroid 50ug once daily for Hypothyroidism (INDICATION). No refills remain on file. Pt is unable to see GP for a new Rx until next month. Pt reports no signs/symptoms of hyper/hypothyroidism or ADR, such as any changes in weight, sleep, energy level, etc. (EFFICACY/SAFETY). As per Netcare, TSH was 2.78 in December 2024 - target of 0.2 - 4.00 as per CPS (EFFICACY). Pt takes medication each morning 30 mins before breakfast and does not miss any doses. A: No concerns noted with current therapy and it is appropriate to continue. Pharmacist to adapt current prescription to support continuity of care. P: RPh to prescribe Synthroid 50ug once daily for 30 days. RPh to contact patient in 30 days to assess efficacy/safety (as noted above). Starting with your next care activity, ensure you have reviewed Standard 7.4 and are collecting IESA to support your processes in providing patient care.

Review sections Section 8.5 and 8.9 of the OAT Guidelines regarding the dilution and established BUDs for methadone carries. when diluting methadone solution, regulated members should record: As discussed: - you can record the dilution of Methadone within the ACP dilution record template (see sample in Appendix 6), within the patient's electronic profile, or directly on the prescription, whichever is most feasible. - all diluted methadone products must be refrigerated, and take-home (carried) doses are permitted a maximum BUD date of 14 days from the date of dilution. A pharmacist must determine a BUD based on the earliest expiry of the ingredients used or 14 days refrigerated, whichever comes first. Confirm with your patient that is prescribed Methdaone that the product is stored within the fridge, thus supporting the 14 days BUD. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidelines_OAT.pdf

In the next 30 days, contact your IT department (or other appropriate party) to update your pharmacy website, social media platforms, etc. with the required information as per the Pharmacy and Drug Regulation Section 23: • A copy of the pharmacy licence. • The location, mailing address, e-mail address and telephone number of the licensed pharmacy. • The name, pharmacist practice permit number, and business address of the licensee. • The following statement: The licensee is required to provide, on the request of a patient, the name and practice permit number of any regulated member who provides a pharmacy service to the patient or who engages in the practice of pharmacy with respect to a patient." • The name and business address of the proprietor. • If the proprietor is a corporation, the name of the proprietor's representative. • A copy or a link to the Patient Concerns Poster As noted, the hours of operations is incorrect, the Pharmacy Licensee/Manager number is the pharmacy License number, and it states that Heidi is the manager/owner. In addition, if you have a 'specific' social media page (e.g. Facebook), then the same information above is required. I believe the Facebook Page for Mint might be for the 'organization' and not for your specific store.

We did not discuss in detail, but I would like you and your team to consider the importance of collecting and documenting the patient's specific goals of therapy. Confirming a patients' goals of therapy will reduce the 'generalization' of goals, allow a more efficient and appropriate follow-up in subsequent interactions, and support your assessment of the safety and efficacy of your interventions/ interactions. As per Standard 1.1.1(b) of the SPPPT, a regulated member must engage with a patient to mutually identify the patient's health needs, goals, and expectations and ensure the patient's input is sought, valued, and included in the decision to provide professional services. Starting with your next patient interaction for you and your team, explore the patients' specific goals of therapy to complement the goals established by clinical practice guidelines. Document these goals to support developing your follow-up/monitoring plan. As an example, a patient's specific goal of therapy could be the return to work with appropriate anxiety control or having the ability to complete the activities of daily living with adequate pain control. At my next visit we will review examples of how this was incorporated. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_SPPPT_New.pdf

We did not discuss this during our visit, thus I will need you confirm that you have an established valid 2/3-way agreement with the patients that you are providing OAT services to. As per Section 5.5 of the OAT guidelines, a pharmacy-patient agreement sets expectations by detailing what the pharmacy team is required to provide as well as the responsibilities for the patient. In some cases, this agreement would include the prescriber. Within the next 30 days, review the profile of each patient that is prescribed OAT and confirm if there is a signed agreement in place. If not, then at the patient interaction have the pharmacist and patient sign the agreement and maintain within the patient’s profile. It is recommended to have blank copies of the Agreement in an OAT specific binder or folder, for easy access for new patients. The OAT agreement can be found as Appendix 3 in the OAT guideline. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidelines_OAT.pdf

We reviewed a few patient profiles at your pharmacy and we noted that there was important patient information missing, such as the patient's medical conditions. The majority of our patients are actively experiencing a drug therapy problem (DTP). A pharmacist will discover As per Standard 7.4.1(c) and (d) of the SPPPT, a pharmacist must consider if there is an actual or potential DTP and the actions that must be taken to address/resolve. In addition, Appendix C of the SPPPT provides more information regarding DTP. The majority of our patients are actively experiencing a drug therapy problem (DTP). A pharmacist will discover these DTPs and respond accordingly if they assess the following: - confirming the indication of any scheduled or unscheduled drugs used by the patient. - assessing the effectiveness of the drugs used by the patient (e.g. BP readings, TSH values, hours of sleep, frequency of PRN use, etc.). - reviewing the common adverse drug reactions of the drugs used by the patient. - assessing the adherence to the drugs used by the patient (e.g. are they taking the medication as prescribed? Are they missing or forgetting to take their medication?). - reviewing Alberta Netcare (e.g. laboratory values, dispensing history, previous antibiotic use, opioid dispensing history, hospital discharge reports, etc.). - establishing an appropriate and specific follow-up/monitoring plan (e.g. when will you follow-up to confirm that the medication resolved the infection or to confirm that they are not experiencing an adverse drug reaction?). By confirming IESA, reviewing Alberta Netcare, and establishing a monitoring plan, you will have the ability to assess DTP risk or if a DTP is actively present. To support you confirming DTPs, refer to the "Vital to Patient Care" ACP tool (shown below). https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CCC_VitaltoPatientCare.pdf https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_SPPPT_New.pdf

When assessing a medication therapy, utilize the SCHOLAR acronym to assist in collecting information that will be used to support the assessment and to assist in your documentation practices. This acronym can be used in many situations, such as confirming appropriateness of antibiotic, when completing an opioid assessment, or for an OTC consult. Here is an example of how SCHOLAR was used for a UTI. S - Symptoms (e.g. pain during urination, lower back pain, blood in urine, etc.). C - Characteristics (e.g. burning pain, stabbing pain, numb pain, etc.) H- History (have they had a UTI in past? Recent infection? Did previous antibiotic treatment work, etc.) O - Onset (When did the UTI start?) L - Location (Where is the pain located? Lower back, kidney area, etc.) A - Aggravating factors (What makes the symptoms worse?) R - Remitting factors (What makes the symptoms better? What antibiotics were used previously that worked?) This will assist a pharmacist in collecting relevant information that will be used to support a prescribing decision, recommendations, follow-up/monitoring plan, etc. As per Standard 7.3 of the SPPPT, a regulated member informs patient care by collecting all APPROPRIATE INFORMATION pertaining to each patient’s healthcare history and current signs, symptoms, and stated health concerns. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_SPPPT_New.pdf

As per Standard 3.6 of the SOLP, a licensee protects patient confidentiality and privacy by ensuring an appropriate space exists within the patient services area for regulated members and other regulated health professionals who are part of the pharmacy team to interact with patients. You have already began work exploring options to resolve this deficiency by introducing a private consultation area by the front entrance. As noted, the requirement of a private consultation area within a community practice is June 1, 2025. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/deadline-approaching-for-private-counselling-room-and-sink- requirements/ At my next visit, we can discuss the status of your renovation. More information regarding renovations can be found below. https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/licensure/managing-my-pharmacy/renovation/

As per Standard 4.8.1 of the SOLP, a licensee must provide a patient access to compounding services a) by directly compounding preparations in the licensed pharmacy in accordance with standards approved by the college respecting the compounding of sterile and non-sterile preparations; or b) through an agreement with a pharmacy issued a compounding and repackaging licence that is in a form that meets the requirements of the Pharmacy and Drug Regulation As confirmed today, your pharmacy does not prepare non-sterile compounds and utilizes pharmacies with compounding and repackaging licenses to provide you the compounded preparations for your patients. As a pharmacy that does not directly compound preparations on site you must complete the 'declaration on compounding services' document and submit to pharmacy@abpharmacy.ca. Completing the required declaration will apply a condition on pharmacy License not permitting your pharmacy to directly compound preparations onsite. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Licensure_DeclarationCompoundingServices.pdf

findings.description_verbatim: As per Standard 4.4.1(b) of the SOLP, a licensee must ensure adequate procedures are in place to identify theft, loss, or diversion of controlled substances, including i. maintaining a perpetual inventory of each controlled substance in stock; ii. a documented audit of the perpetual inventories to verify accuracy A. at least every three months, and B. each time there is a change in proprietor or licensee; iii. investigating, resolving, and documenting any discrepancies identified; iv. evaluating whether procedure changes or preventative measures are required to prevent future discrepancies; and v. reporting any loss or theft of controlled substances to Health Canada within 10 days of discovery. Your current process consists of counting all narcotic and controlled substances monthly, and if a particular medication was not dispensed since the last count, staff do not count the product and refer to the quantity from the last month. As noted in the Standard above, you need to demonstrate that the perpetual inventory of every medication is being completed every three months. I expressed concern that your current process may not fulfill the Standard, so I will ask that you consider how to ensure that every drug is counted quarterly, especially if you did not dispense a particularly drug for greater than three months. We can discuss at my next visit. Continue to document the personnel completing the narcotic count and the date the count was completed. If there are discrepancies, explore where they may have occurred and report any unexplained loss or theft of narcotic or controlled drugs to Health Canada within 10 days of discovery. This process is now completed electronically, which you register for within Health Canada's website. Of note, you must keep all records on-site for two years if they contain information which must be retained under the relevant regulations. https://abpharmacy.ca/articles/managing-your-controlled-substances https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/health-concerns/controlled-substances- precursor-chemicals/controlled-substances/compliance-monitoring/loss-theft.html https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/publications/healthy-living/loss-theft- controlled-substances-precursors/reporting-guideline-process.html#a34 https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_SOLP_New.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

The majority of our patients are actively experiencing a drug therapy problem (DTP). A pharmacist will discover As per Standard 7.4.1(c) and (d) of the SPPPT, a pharmacist must consider if there is an actual or potential DTP and the actions that must be taken to address/resolve. In addition, Appendix C of the SPPPT provides more information regarding DTP. The majority of our patients are actively experiencing a drug therapy problem (DTP). A pharmacist will discover these DTPs and respond accordingly if they assess the following: - confirming the indication of any scheduled or unscheduled drugs used by the patient. - assessing the effectiveness of the drugs used by the patient (e.g. BP readings, TSH values, hours of sleep, frequency of PRN use, etc.). - reviewing the common adverse drug reactions of the drugs used by the patient. - assessing the adherence to the drugs used by the patient (e.g. are they taking the medication as prescribed? Are they missing or forgetting to take their medication?). - reviewing Alberta Netcare (e.g. laboratory values, dispensing history, previous antibiotic use, opioid dispensing history, hospital discharge reports, etc.). - establishing an appropriate and specific follow-up/monitoring plan (e.g. when will you follow-up to confirm that the medication resolved the infection or to confirm that they are not experiencing an adverse drug reaction?). By confirming IESA, reviewing Alberta Netcare, and establishing a monitoring plan, you will have the ability to assess DTP risk or if a DTP is actively present. To support you confirming DTPs, refer to the "Vital to Patient Care" ACP tool (shown below). https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CCC_VitaltoPatientCare.pdf

As you informed me, you do not have a particular website for your pharmacy, but the medical clinic that you share a premise with lists your pharmacy within their overall website. As noted, this website has not been updated since 2018. Any online platforms, including websites or social media applications, that promote a pharmacy operation, must fulfill the requirements as per the Pharmacy and Drug Regulation (Section 23) noted below. Couple options to resolve this deficiency. 1.) Have the medical clinic remove your pharmacy from their website. 2.) Have the medical clinic update their website to include the requirements below, and ensure that it is updated annually with your new Pharmacy License. Pharmacy and Drug Regulation Section 23: • A copy of the pharmacy licence. • The location, mailing address, e-mail address and telephone number of the licensed pharmacy. • The name, pharmacist practice permit number, and business address of the licensee. • The following statement: The licensee is required to provide, on the request of a patient, the name and practice permit number of any regulated member who provides a pharmacy service to the patient or who engages in the practice of pharmacy with respect to a patient." • The name and business address of the proprietor. • If the proprietor is a corporation, the name of the proprietor's representative. • A copy or a link to the Patient Concerns Poster At my next visit, we can discuss the direction you took.

Today you provided me an update on a few items: - You have recently sold your community pharmacy practice, with new proprietors in place starting July 1, 2025. - Renovation plans for establishing a private consultation area, as addressed during my first visit, is currently on pause as per request of the new proprietors who will want to establish their plans themselves. - You showed me updates to your policy and procedure manual. Since a new proprietor will be in place prior to my third visit, we agreed that you would confirm the plan with your pharmacy's policies and procedures. Will you maintain your current manual or will you introduce the new proprietor's organizational manual? If you will be utilizing your current manual, further updates are required which we will review in more detail at my next visit. If using the new proprietor's organizational policies, we will need to review to confirm that it aligns with your pharmacy practice. We will discuss further at my next visit.

During my visit today, you provide me more details of the transition of the ownership of the pharmacy to Mint Health. Currently, there is not specific timeline when the required renovations will be explored by the new ownership group to address the lack of a private consultation area, which was first discussed at the end of March 2025. To address the deficiency of not having a private consultation area, which is required per Standard 3.6 of the SOLP, it is an expectation that a renovation application will be submitted to ACP prior to August 15, 2025. Failure to reach this timeline will lead to a 4th visit and a re-inspection fee of $912. In addition, since the name of the pharmacy has changed, all signage associated to the previous pharmacy name needs to be replaced to avoid any confusion with the public as indicated within Standard 3.8 of the SOLP and Section 24(1) of the Pharmacy and Drug Regulation. Please keep me posted on how things proceed.

findings.description_verbatim: At the time of the inspection, your anaphylaxis kit only contained a 0.3 mg epinephrine autoinjector and you indicated that you do not administer injections to children. We discussed the current Public Health Agency of Canada epinephrine dosing recommendations, and how 0.5mg is recommended for patients 46kg and above / older than 12 years (see Table 4 in the link below). During the inspection you added epinephrine ampoules to your kit to ensure you have dosing required for these patients. We also identified that the epinephrine dosing recommendations posted in your private consult room are outdated. Continue to ensure your anaphylaxis kit is equipped with an appropriate range of epinephrine/autoinjectors based on the weights of the patients to whom you administer injections. Update your epinephrine dosing recommendations with the reference listed below. Please email Kiran (kiran.juma@abpharmacy.ca) digital evidence demonstrating correction. https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/publications/healthy-living/canadian- immunization-guide-part-2-vaccine-safety/page-4-early-vaccine-reactions-including- anaphylaxis.html

There was no evidence of documentation of calibration activities performed for your weigh scale. Ensure that your weigh scale is calibrated with an external weight according to manufacturer specifications (at least quarterly and when the scale is moved) and that this activity is documented in a log. When documenting calibration activities for your scale, ensure to document additional details about the outcome (for instance, document the weight used for calibration, what the reading was, and whether any corrective action was required). Please email Kiran (kiran.juma@abpharmacy.ca) digital evidence demonstrating correction.

During the inspection you indicated that you provide wardstock medications regularly to the clinic across the street. Medications provided include Schedule 1 medications such as amoxicillin/ clavulanic acid, lorazepam, Sublocade (buprenorphine extended release injection), etc. Some of these medications are repackaged by Mint Health + Drugs: Sherwood Park (3556) who you indicated has a compounding and repackaging license. You did not have a compounding and repackaging agreement in place with this pharmacy. We also reviewed draft policies and procedures in your manual that involve providing wardstock medications to paramedics. You clarified that this is not applicable to your practice. We discussed that the Standards of Practice do not support pharmacists serving a purely distributive role. Pharmacists must be meaningfully involved in the care of patients, including establishing a professional relationship and completing a fulsome assessment prior to dispensing medications. Records must accurately reflect the care provided by the pharmacy professionals. Selling drugs “for office use” is reserved for emergencies or infrequent situation and is not intended to authorize wholesaling of drugs on an ongoing basis as part of the supply chain. In order to sell drugs on an ongoing, regular basis, the pharmacy would need to acquire a Drug Establishment License from Health Canada, in addition to a Controlled Drugs and Substances Dealer’s License if controlled substances are involved. Furthermore, we discussed that Mint Health + Drugs: Sherwood (3566) does not have a compounding and repackaging license, thus is not authorized to repackage medications for other pharmacies. We also discussed the ACP compounding and repackaging agreement template, including section 2.4 that refers to patient specific prescriptions. In light of the information above, implement changes to your pharmacy practice to ensure all standards, regulations and legislation are being met, specifically with respect to dispensing, patient assessment, and record keeping. Consider options we discussed (e.g. obtaining patient specific prescriptions and dispensing medications directly to patients). Moving forward: 1) Discontinue your practice of providing wardstock medications to other health care professionals (physicians, paramedic, etc.) 2) Review the Link article below and ensure the practice of ordering and receiving repackaged medications from Mint Health + Drugs: Sherwood (3566) is ceased immediately https://abpharmacy.ca/news/purchasing-or-selling-compounded-or-repackaged-drugs/ Please email Kiran (kiran.juma@abpharmacy.ca) digital evidence demonstrating correction.

findings.description_verbatim: At the moment, the pharmacy is ready to provide Point of Care Testing for strep testing. We discussed reviewing the ACP Point of Care Testing Standards (https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/ default/files/Standards_Lab_POCT.pdf). Policies and Procedures (P&P) have not been created for these POCT. We discussed that the P&P must outline: a. purpose of the test; b. specimen collection, identification, and handling; c. preparation of reagents and other materials; d. calibration of equipment (instrument/reagent) as required by the manufacturer; e. quality control procedures including ensuring reagents, materials, and equipment are stable and not expired; f. stepwise instructions for use; g. training requirements and procedures; h. personal protective equipment (PPE); i. special alerts to out-of-control and “critical result” values; j. disposal of waste; k. cleaning of contaminated surfaces and equipment; l. limitations of the test and procedure; m. establishment of acceptable target or reference ranges for QC materials; n. remedial action for out-of-control readings; o. reference interval (“normal values”) for populations being treated; p. reagent, test unit, and material storage; q. validation/verification of device/equipment; r. action required if test system is inoperable; s. adverse event protocols related to all phases of testing; t. reporting and documentation of results; and, u. criteria for referral of specimens to an accredited laboratory. Template for documenting the POCT P&P can also be found in the ACP General Pharmacy P&P. Template found here: https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ Policies_Procedures_Template.docx In the next 30 days, please create P&P for strep testing. Please set aside for review at the next visit.

findings.description_verbatim: 1. We discussed your current data backup procedure, and at the time of our visit you were not certain how you would retrieve the backup in the event of data loss. Over the next 30 days, speak with the appropriate parties to develop an understanding of your data backup/retrieval processes, and update this accordingly in your P&P manual. 2. We discussed that you have started shredding hardcopies after scanning them in. We reviewed the Kroll "missing images" report during the assessment and identified this report as an effective tool in assessing compliance with scanning procedures in your pharmacy. Discuss this report with your team and implement a process for using it regularly moving forward. We will review this report at the next visit to ensure transaction records are not missing scans.

We noted that there was no calibration date noted on the thermometer or probe for your stock fridge. We discussed the importance of being able to verify the accuracy of your temperature readings to ensure your refrigerated medications are being appropriately stored. Within the next 30 days, obtain a new thermometer or updated calibration for this device. Date State Category Due Date Personal Responsible Completed Date

We discussed your current process to verify/correct any missed uploads to Netcare, and you noted it would typically be done monthly with about 100 items to correct in that period of time. We agreed to move to a daily or weekly review process instead to minimize the pile-up of response errors. Update this accordingly in your P&P manual. We will review progress at my next visit.

1. With regards to delivery of medications, we reviewed your current process of tracking prescriptions being sent to patients, and discussed the importance of being able to maintain a complete audit trail for each prescription, from the time of entry to the time of pickup/delivery. Over the next 30 days, please update your delivery logging process to ensure it is clear upon review as to which specific medications were delivered. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/storing-and-delivering-prepared-prescriptions/ 2. We also discussed the recent amendments to the Pharmacy and Drug Regulation on June 1, 2022, whereby pharmacy licensees must ensure a criminal record check is obtained from all unregulated individuals engaged by the pharmacy to support or provide assistance in providing a restricted activity or the delivery of a drug to a patient. You noted this has been completed for your pharmacy assistant, but not for delivery drivers. Within 30 days, please obtain any missing criminal record checks, retain documentation on file, and develop a plan for how often these checks will be repeated. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/criminal-record-checks-unregulated-pharmacy-staff/ Date State Category Due Date Personal Responsible Completed Date

You noted that you do not prepare any compounds on site, as you order everything through your C&R pharmacy. We briefly discussed that all pharmacies are required to meet Level A requirements. Review the resources on the non-sterile compounding page below. We will discuss this further at my next visit. https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/practice-resources/pharmacy-compounding/non- sterile-compounding/#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20must%20meet%20Level,are%20compliant %20with%20these%20standards. Date State Category Due Date Personal Responsible Completed Date

We reviewed your practices for providing certain clinical services by phone, and discussed the Standards of Practice for Virtual Care, which can be found at the following link: https:// abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Standards_Virtual.pdf As discussed, these standards identify that virtual care can only be utilized if it complements the care an individual regularly receives in-person at the pharmacy, is deemed appropriate in that specific situation (and within the allowance outlined in the Standards), and consent is obtained. Consider any impacts these standards have on your existing practice, particularly with respect to the use of the telephone to communicate with patients. If any care is being provided virtually, ensure that all of the standards are observed and documented on the patient record (see sections 10, 11, and 12). Over the next 30 days, update your policies & procedures indicating how you will achieve and maintain compliance with these standards, and come up with a plan to begin appropriately documenting that virtual care was provided.

We discussed the value of documenting the rationale for your prescribing decision, including how you decided on a certain medication, dosing regimen, route of administration, etc. Citing any references or resources consulted as part of prescribing may help justify the clinical decision, in addition to any patient-specific factors considered. Date State Category Due Date Personal Responsible Completed Date

findings.description_verbatim: 1. We discussed that you would store unserviceable controlled inventory in your safe, separate from inventory which may be dispensed. Going forward, we reiterated the importance of maintaining an accurate perpetual inventory of the unserviceable stock (i.e. a running tally of the items pending destruction) such that when the item is removed from the active inventory and taken out of Kroll, you still maintain an understanding of how much stock you have in the pharmacy. Furthermore ensure this is verified again at the time of destruction. 2. We reviewed your local narcotic destruction log, and discussed some items which are missing on it, including the method of destruction and a joint statement indicating that the items were rendered inconsumable by the regulated members. Please refer to Section 3.1.3 of the Health Canada guidance document linked below to review the record-keeping requirements for local destruction of unserviceable controlled medications. You also indicated you accept post-consumer returns containing controlled substances; I have linked the guidance document for that below as well, as your records for post-consumer return destruction must be kept separate from your unserviceable controlled inventory. Over the next 30 days, please update your processes accordingly, and we will review them at our next visit. https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/health-concerns/controlled-substances- precursor-chemicals/controlled-substances/compliance-monitoring/compliance-monitoring- controlled-substances/unserviceable-stock.html#a3_13 https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/health-concerns/controlled-substances- precursor-chemicals/controlled-substances/compliance-monitoring/compliance-monitoring- controlled-substances/post-consumer-returns.html 3. We discussed that you are currently destroying controlled substances with your unregulated pharmacy assistant as the witness. We discussed Health Canada's requirement that destruction can only be completed by two regulated members. Ensure your process is updated going forward.

We reviewed examples of quarterly drug incident reviews completed over the last year, and noted some examples of reviews which did not have any incidents. We discussed the purpose of the quarterly review as a reflective practice to consider systems and practices which impact quality assurance in the pharmacy. If there are no drug incidents, consider any near misses, positive practices, or other trends which you have noticed, and reflect on what areas for improvement there may be as you move forward. Over the next 30 days, implement a process to begin tracking near misses. We will review the quarterly review from Q1 2024 at my next visit. Date State Category Due Date Personal Responsible Completed Date

We discussed the value of documenting specific details regarding your patient assessment, such as BP reading numbers instead of "his normal BP". Continue to work towards gathering and documenting those details; we will review additional examples at my next visit.

Document training and skills assessment checklists any compounding personnel, or at least yourself as the compounding supervisor. Review Section 6.2 of the non-sterile compounding guidance document (indicating items to include in your MFR), and develop at least ONE master formulation record, to complement the risk assessment you documented under #2 above. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidance_Non-sterile_Compounding.pdf https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ Compounding_TemplateMasterFormulationRecord.pdf 4. As per standard 6.6 of the Standards for the Operation of Licensed Pharmacies , the licensee must , at least quarterly, review any drug error reports with all regulated members and employees that work in the prescription department. Every incident is a learning opportunity, and promoting discussions regarding practice changes or preventative measures will improve safety and mitigate the chances of a similar incident in the future. The ACP quarterly review form can be used to document this required QA step: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/DrugIncidentQuarterlyReview_Fillable.pdf

You are currently providing drugs to the Alex clinics without patient specific prescriptions ("for clinic/office use"). As per Guidance on Drug Establishment Licences, the drug establishment licensing requirements apply to wholesale of schedule 1 drugs. We discussed that the Standards of Practice do not allow for prescriptions to be dispensed without the pharmacist's assessment of the patient. Your sale of drugs to the Alex clinics is considered a wholesaling activity as it is outside of a pharmacist/patient relationship. If “office use” sales are occurring on an ongoing regular basis outside of an emergency situation, then the pharmacy would need to acquire a Drug Establishment License from Health Canada to wholesale. In the next 30 days, develop a transition plan with the Alex clinic to ensure this activity ceases in the absence of a drug establishment license. Ensure that all medications are dispensed with a patient-specific prescription. Some potential solutions include the healthcare professional obtaining drugs directly from drug wholesalers themselves. If for emergency use, ACP council has defined an emergency as a circumstance where a patient urgently requires a professional service that includes a restricted activity for the purposes of preventing imminent mortality or morbidity. https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/drugs-health-products/compliance- enforcement/establishment-licences/directives-guidance-documents-policies/guidance-drug- establishment-licences-drug-establishment-licensing-fees-0002.html

You mentioned that the pharmacy provides hazardous injections (testosterone, methotrexate, other hormonal injections) to patients of the pharmacy. As a review, ensure that multi-dose vials are discarded within 28 days unless stated otherwise by the manufacturer. We identified that your pharmacy has not established all processes in accordance with the Guidelines for Medication and Vaccine Injection Safety. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidelines_InjectionSafety.pdf Prior to providing injections for hazardous products, ensure you are meeting requirements outlined in Guideline #6. Copied here for your convenience: 6. If hazardous drugs are administered in the practice setting they are stored, handled and used safely. a. A written procedure is in place for the management of hazardous drugs. b. Hazardous drugs are stored separately from other medications if possible. c. Employees and others at risk from handling hazardous drugs are identified and provided with adequate training and equipment. d. Cytotoxic spill kits are available and staff are trained in spill management e. Adequate personal protective equipment including but not limited to gloves, disposable gowns and facial protection is worn for administration of hazardous drugs. i. Additional personal protective equipment is used where there is potential for splash, spill or aerosolization. (Consider this link: https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/docs/2023-130/2023-130.pdf? id=10.26616/NIOSHPUB2023130) ii. Personal protective equipment is disposable where possible. f. Hazardous drug waste and equipment is disposed at point‐of‐use into a cytotoxic waste container with minimal manipulation (e.g. needles and syringes are left intact). Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date
