We discussed some ways of documenting patient specific goals of therapy by modifying the general goals of therapy that are part of the template. Going forward, remember to document patient specific goals of therapy on the patient record.

1. We reviewed the documentation requirements for follow-ups and other clinical services completed via virtual care. Based on our discussion, it was unclear if the patient record for the virtual assessments consistently contained documentation of informed consent and the time, date and enabling technology used to complete the assessment, along with rationale for providing virtual care. We discussed the requirement for this documentation on the patient record for each virtual patient assessment. Starting with the next virtual care assessment, please document informed consent, rationale, and the time, date and enabling technology used on the patient record. 2. Occasionally, patient records (prescription images) are sent over email by the patients. We discussed the following resource: https://abpharmacy.ca/news/prescription-transmission-through-apps-and-online-tools/ Going forward, ensure that medications are not prepared prior to receiving an original prescription. We discussed that a Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) must be submitted for all enabling technology to the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Alberta (OIPC). Additionally, we discussed that this technology may not be secure to transmit health information should the patient decide to communicate their health information through this method. In the next 30 days, please ensure all enabling technology is approved by the OIPC through a PIA. Please set aside for review at the next visit.

Today we reviewed an example of medication extension (completed 2 days ago). You mentioned that the assessment has not been documented on the patient record. We discussed the importance of documenting all elements of data (patient specific goals of therapy, relevant medication and medical history, Netcare lab results, signs and symptoms, etc), assessment (indication, effectiveness, safety, adherence, rationale for choosing therapy, references assessed, etc), and plan (person responsible for follow-up, specific monitoring parameters, expected outcomes, appropriate timeframes, etc). Additionally, you may consider implementing the SCHOLAR acronym (Symptoms, Characteristics, History, Onset, Location, Aggravating Factors, Remitting Factors) as a tool to assist in gathering and documenting relevant information regarding signs and symptoms. We also discussed some ways to use a template to document medication extensions, especially given the current busy flu season, to ensure documentation is completed in a timely manner. Going forward, ensure that clinical documentation is completed in a timely manner (for ex: by end of the day).

category.raw: Non-sterile Compounding : Training and Skills Assessment

findings.description_verbatim: You've established a thorough training and skills assessment program for your compounding personnel. However, we identified a few areas that, while they may be covered during the training and assessment program, aren't consistently documented on the record of the staff member. These include: - procedures for cleaning compounding equipment and surfaces, including the types and order of agents to use for cleaning - procedures for using safety equipment, in particular the eyewash station - procedures for maintaining compounding equipment, in particular calibrating the compounding scale Aim to have these procedures updated in your manual prior to my next assessment, and ensure they are implemented at your next staff training and skills assessment.

category.raw: Laboratory and Point- of-Care Testing : Point- of-Care Testing Program

findings.description_verbatim: Please ensure that any future point-of-care test (strep testing, COVID testing, etc.) offered at your pharmacy has a comprehensive set of policies and procedures developed. Please include information to address the following sections of Standard 16: • Screening and assessment activities • Specimen collection, identification, and handling. • Preparation of reagents and other materials. • Calibration of equipment (instrument/reagent) as required by the manufacturer. • Stepwise instructions for use. • Training requirements and procedures. I would suggest that training should also include a review of the device user manual(s). • Personal protective equipment (PPE). Your SOP should include information regarding mask usage. • Cleaning of contaminated surfaces and equipment. Please note the differences between cleaning and disinfection. • Limitations of the test and procedure. • Establishment of acceptable target or reference ranges for QC materials. For example, control testing instructions. • Reagent, test unit, and material storage. • Action required if test system is inoperable. • Adverse event protocols related to all phases of testing. • Care planning and monitoring considerations • Collaboration requirements follow test completion Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed new standards pertaining to equity, diversity, inclusion (EDI); accessibility; cultural sensitivity, and Indigenous cultural safety (SPPPT domain 1). Each pharmacy needs to have standard operating procedures (SOPs) to support the pharmacy team in incorporating these concepts into practice. We discussed some patient groups who may be subject to barriers to care due to prejudice, physical or cognitive barriers, etc. Some key considerations for you to consider include: -Changes that can be made in the pharmacy to be more inclusive for these groups -Changes to make the space more welcoming (e.g., language, pharmacy appearance, physical environment) -Staff training and ongoing monitoring to ensure implementation of these changes. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/treating-people-with-respect-and-dignity/ Encourage team members to review the article in the Link above and begin to reflect on your current approaches for meeting EDI, accessibility, cultural sensitivity and Indigenous cultural safety expectations in your pharmacy.

category.raw: Pharmacy Practice : Monitoring and Follow- up

findings.description_verbatim: We reviewed a prescribing example for valacyclovir where the pharmacist's monitoring and follow up plan was not documented on the record of care. Work as a team to ensure that a documented and patient-specific monitoring plan is included within the summary note for all care interventions (prescription extension, adaptation, care plan, etc),. The monitoring plan (i.e. road map) should identify who is following up, when this is expected to occur, and what parameters are being assessed at that time. These details must be identified even if the follow up to an intervention is delegated to another health provider or to the patient themselves. Set aside an example of a care plan and care plan follow up for us to review at the next assessment. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

Prior to my next assessment, please replace or replenish your existing eyewash equipment as discussed during the assessment. Aim to apply an expiry or beyond-use date to the new supply when installed.

At the time of this assessment, you did not have any active patients receiving opioid agonist therapy at your pharmacy. We will continue to review the status of such services at upcoming assessments to ensure compliance with relevant standards and guidelines.

Remote verbal assessments are happening over the phone for patients that regularly visit your pharmacy for things like clinical follow ups and prescription extensions. We discussed some of the basic requirements to consider for this type of virtual care, including: 1. Identifying yourself and your role 2. Confirming the identity of the patient 3. Ensuring the patient is in a private setting 4. Obtaining informed consent from the patient to proceed with sharing patient information over the phone (this should be documented in the patient record) 5. Documenting the consent in the patient record 6. Provide the patient with contact information for follow up You've indicated that the above elements are not always communicated to patients or documented on the record of care. Please review these requirements with all team members and monitor these activities for compliance moving forward. Document how you will provide and document virtual care activities in your policy manual as well. We will review progress on this item at the next assessment. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

findings.description_verbatim: We reviewed a few examples of patient assessments, including initial access prescribing, and opioid assessments. We discussed the value of adding more specific details to your assessment and documentation, such as gathering more information about the condition (e.g. using SCHOLAR - where is the pain specifically located, how long has it been occurring, etc), specifically discussing their goals of therapy (e.g. using a pain scale; considering functional goals such as going to work, playing with kids, etc), and documenting what you are looking for & finding on Netcare (e.g. no Hx of polypharmacy, reviewed eGFR, uric acid, etc). Continue to work on incorporating details such as these into your clinical interactions going forward.

At the time of our visit, we noted tramadol-containing products on the drug wall, as well as codeine in a Pacmed container on your shelf. As per Standard 5.14 of the Standards for the Operation of Licensed Pharmacies, all schedule 1 narcotics and all drugs designated as type 1 medications by the Tracked Prescription Program Alberta (TPP) must be stored in a metal safe that is secured in place and equipped with a time delay lock set at a minimum of five minutes. Going forward, ensure that your medications are stored securely and in manner outlined in the Standards for the Operation of Licensed Pharmacies.

We reviewed your pharmacy's use of Healthbox signage and the computer station. You noted that no physician care is being provided (virtually, or otherwise) in your pharmacy space, and that the webcam in your counseling room is only used for internal meetings (not for patient consults). We reviewed some of the considerations surrounding this topic, including shared premise and ensuring advertising is not misleading for patients. Additionally, one of your assistants had mentioned in her description of the Healthbox platform that it allows for patients to book virtual consults with physicians from your counseling room; please ensure you discuss this with your team to ensure consistent messaging is being provided to patients, and that the pharmacy space is only utilized for pharmacy services. From previous email communication with Vince: I hope your day is going well. I am writing to discuss regarding the Health Box website (https:// healthbox.ca/) that appears to be linked to your pharmacy. I recall that at your most recent inspection, we discussed your recent renovation and the existence of the Health Box kiosk near your drop-off area. At that time, you reported that the kiosk would act as an information center and a resource for general health information for patients. Please refer to my comments below: 1. The website advertises that "Health Box is a new clinic solution powered by Mint Health + Drugs providing accessible services to those who need it most. We have a highly trained team of pharmacists, medical professionals, and partnered virtual physicians with an unwavering commitment to the welfare of our patients". As per the following document on pharmacy walk-in clinics (https://abpharmacy.ca/articles/walk-pharmacy-clinics), if a pharmacy team chooses to use the concept of “clinic” when identifying or advertising their pharmacy, they must hold themselves out as a pharmacy and differentiate their services from that of a medical clinic. If the term “clinic” or “walk-in clinic” is used in the advertising for a pharmacy, the word “pharmacy” must also be used and all signage and advertisements must be clear that the walk-in service provided by the pharmacy team is part of the licensed pharmacy. In addition, as outlined in my previous email, you must submit a renovation application to the ACP registration department for your renovation to add two new counseling rooms that are being used to provide pharmacy services. 2. The website also outlines that Health Box has "a highly trained team of physicians, medical professionals, diagnostic tools and an unwavering commitment to the welfare of our patients" and lists RocketDoctor and tiahealth as partnered virtual physicians. If a prescription department shares premises with another health professional or business, then the prescription department must operate as a lock and leave pharmacy. This would include virtual physician services being provided within your pharmacy. Review Requirements #3 and #4 of the Foundational Requirements and implement changes to ensure compliance to the Foundational Requirements (https:// abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/FoundationalRequirementsGuidanceDocument.pdf). 3. If Health Box kiosk collects and processes any patient/health information, an updated Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) must be completed and submitted to the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner (OIPC). As discussed during the most recent inspection, an updated PIA must be completed and submitted to OIPC before using any new technology handling patient information.

You mentioned that all the pharmacists on the team currently access the required references through the University of Alberta library, with access received as a result of volunteering to precept students. This does not meet the requirement of having site-specific access which would be available to everyone working at the pharmacy. Furthermore, we discussed that this system doesn't have access to the Martindale database (or another foreign drug database). You have indicated that you have access to certain reference subscriptions on a corporate level, which may cover the requirements. Within the next 30 days, please obtain all required references for your pharmacy, and submit digital evidence of these to Anuvir (anuvir.bhullar@abpharmacy.ca). The list of required references can be found on the ACP website here: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ RequiredReferences.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed the current practice in place of regularly providing drugs to local fire departments in an "office use" capacity, whereby there is no patient-specific prescription. You noted that while you have made progress with certain locations, there are still some departments which have been unable to source their own supplies so far. It was reiterated at our visit that the Standards of Practice do not allow for prescriptions to be dispensed without the pharmacist's assessment of the patient. We agreed on a timeline of 6 weeks to completely cease this practice, which will allow sufficient time for the departments which have not found alternative supply to do so. You may consider referring the remaining departments to contact those which have already sourced alternative supply, or to contact their regulatory body for more resources. Upon resolution of this deficiency, please email Anuvir (anuvir.bhullar@abpharmacy.ca) to confirm. Additionally, we discussed the previous practice of supplying drugs to another community pharmacy in medication strips. As your pharmacy does not have a C&R license, this repackaging practice would not be permitted. You have noted that this is no longer being done. From previous email communication with Vince: You are currently providing drugs to the local fire department without patient specific prescriptions ("for clinic/office use"). As per Guidance on Drug Establishment Licences, the drug establishment licensing requirements apply to wholesale of schedule 1 drugs. Your sale of drugs to the fire hall is considered a wholesaling activity as it is not at retail and outside of a pharmacist/patient relationship. If “office use” sales are occurring on an ongoing regular basis outside of an emergency situation, then the pharmacy would need to acquire a Drug Establishment License from Health Canada to wholesale. The drug we reviewed during the visit was a controlled substance, therefore also requires a license dealer permit from Health Canada, except for in emergency situations (refer to the articles below). In the next 30 days, develop a transition plan with the local fire department to ensure this activity ceases in the absence of a drug establishment license/licensed dealer permit. Ensure that all medications are dispensed with a patient-specific prescription. Some potential solutions include the fire hall/healthcare professional obtaining drugs directly from drug wholesalers themselves. If for emergency use, ACP council has defined an emergency as a circumstance where a patient urgently requires a professional service that includes a restricted activity for the purposes of preventing imminent mortality or morbidity. https://www.canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/drugs-health-products/compliance- enforcement/establishment-licences/directives-guidance-documents-policies/guidance-drug- establishment-licences-drug-establishment-licensing-fees-0002.html https://abpharmacy.ca/articles/sale-controlled-substances-between-pharmacies-not-permitted

findings.description_verbatim: The higher prescription volume at this location, along with the current flu-shot season, can make it challenging for one pharmacist to have the time to engage with patients and provide clinical care. There are also safety concerns with respect to unintentional medication errors and incidents. In our discussion of the quarterly drug errors, there was one error which occurred as a result of a heavy workload for the pharmacist. Over the next month please have a discussion with your district manager / proprietor to ensure you have sufficient pharmacy personnel scheduled each day, including pharmacists, to be able to provide quality clinical care, to meet all standards of practice, and to ensure patient safety is not compromised.

1. Medical dictionary was not available in the pharmacy at the time of the visit. Please review: https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/RequiredReferences.pdf In the next 30 days, please ensure the pharmacy contains a medical dictionary reference as mentioned in the link above. 2.. We continued our discussion regarding the virtual care practice occurring in the pharmacy. As per Standard 3d(i) of the Standards of Practice for Virtual Care (SPVC), virtual care must optimize and complement the in-person care the patient regularly receives from the regulated member. We also reviewed Standard 3d(ii) and 3d(iii) which outlines the scenarios where virtual care may be provided to a patient who does not receive in person care from the regulated member. We noted how these scenarios would only apply in limited circumstances where the rationale for providing virtual care should be documented. Since the previous visit, you have started to create a protocol to determine rationale for providing virtual care travel assessments. This protocol indicates the limited availability of the onsite pharmacist (ex: onsite pharmacist only available for travel appointments 1-2 days per week) as a rationale for providing virtual travel assessments. We discussed how these scenarios would not comply with SPVC as they would not satisfy the conditions outlined in Standards 3d(ii) and 3d(iii). For this protocol, we discussed expanding on and clarifying circumstances (other than limited time for onsite pharmacist) that may require the patient to receive virtual travel assessment, as per Standard 3d(ii) and 3d(iii). Over the next month, continue to discuss implications of these standards with the pharmacy team and modify the current protocol to align with these standards.

Two of the medication extensions patient record we reviewed today did not appear to contain documentation of relevant information gathered from Netcare. We discussed gathering and documenting all relevant Netcare findings on the patient record to assist in assessing appropriateness of each therapy. Starting with the next clinical service, please document all relevant findings from Netcare on the patient record. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

One of the examples of medication adaptation we reviewed today did not appear to contain indication for use of Twinrix. We reviewed the importance of documenting indication of therapy in the patient record to assist in assessing medication appropriateness and ongoing care. Going forward, remember to consistently document indication for use on the patient record.

We reviewed the current process of documenting clinical services provided virtually and identified the following findings: 1. Documentation of patient's consent for the use of virtual care is not being documented on the patient record. Additionally, it appears that the patients are not aware of the location where the pharmacist is providing the virtual care. We discussed the requirement for documentation of patient consent, including the location of virtual pharmacist, on the patient record. These requirements are also reflected in the pharmacy's Virtual Care Policies and Procedures. As part of documenting consent for use of virtual care, you mentioned that a statement will be created and shared with the patients prior to each virtual assessment outlining the pharmacist's name, location, limitation of virtual care, potential privacy concerns, technological challenges, and any other information related to use of virtual care. In the next 10 days, please create a process to document consent for use of virtual care for each virtual interaction on the patient record. 2. We discussed the process of transferring care of the patient from the pharmacist that provides virtual care to onsite pharmacist. The clinical documentation created by the virtual care pharmacist does not consistently contain responsibilities of onsite pharmacist. We discussed the value of including this information as part of follow-up and monitoring parameters to ensure a clear plan is in place for transition of care between pharmacists. Going forward, remember to include documentation of transfer of patient care on the patient record.

findings.description_verbatim: The examples of travel consult and medication adaptation/extensions we reviewed today did not contain documentation of a follow-up and monitoring plan. We discussed that while the pharmacist verbally discusses the follow-up plan with the patient, this plan must be documented on the patient record to assist in ongoing care. We discussed that the follow-up plan would detail who (person responsible for follow-up), what (exact monitoring parameters and expected outcomes), and when (appropriate timeframes). Starting with the next clinical interaction, remember to document the follow-up and monitoring plan on the patient record.

We noted that there are narcotics within your automated dispenser. In the next 30 days, ensure that your medications are stored securely and in manner outlined in the Standards for the Operation of Licensed Pharmacies. https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ACP_SOLP.pdf
